runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 4 in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) elasticity survey
ETI estimates commonly used in tax-benefit microsimulation work
standard labor-supply calibration practice for primary earners
runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the middle-lower part of the earnings distribution, I expect a modest positive substitution response: larger than near-zero short-run intensive-margin responses often used for high earners, but still well below 1.0. Decile 4 should be somewhat responsive to tax-benefit incentives, yet constrained by labor-market attachment and hours frictions.

citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) elasticities-of-taxable-income overview

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in a middle-lower earnings decile, I expect modest but nontrivial substitution responsiveness: below top-income ETI-style values, but above near-zero participation-only responses.
